title: Redirect Semantics, Method Rewriting, Credential Scope, and Loop Control · Go Client Systems · Goroutine Forensics
summary: An on-call reliability engineer tracing a production client incident must isolate a plausible but incorrect result from conflicting traces, repair its cause, and retain a regression witness for redirect semantics, method rewriting, credential scope, and loop control, producing auditable redirect policy engine. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a Go 1.26 net/http auditable redirect policy engine and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress…
objectives: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.

Resolve Location references against the current request URL and validate the resulting destination each hop. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.

Bound redirect hops, detect loops, retain the chain, and expose the policy decision to callers. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Go Order the regression proof · Debug Manual Redirect Defense
steps.instruction:
Order the Manual Redirect Defense Go HTTP evidence cycle for this debug task before implementing it.

Evidence target: Go Order the regression proof · Debug Manual Redirect Defense.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Manual Redirect Defense assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward. For Go Order the regression proof · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, keep Manual Redirect Defense connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.text: Go incident collection agent sends POST G-5 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Choose automatic, manual, or rejected redirects from method replay, SSRF, credential, audit, and user-consent risk. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Manual Redirect Defense has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: For Go Order the regression proof · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Manual Redirect Defense precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Go Order the regression proof · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: Go incident collection agent sends POST G-5 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Choose automatic, manual, or rejected redirects from method replay, SSRF, credential, audit, and user-consent risk. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.

For Go Order the regression proof · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Following redirects is transport plumbing with no application or security semantics. This remains false for Go 1.26 net/http.

options.text: Declare Go method semantics, target authority, credentials, body ownership, and byte and time budgets for the debug Manual Redirect Defense case
Predict Go resolution, transport, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup outcomes for the debug Manual Redirect Defense case
Run the smallest deterministic Go request or response model and retain inspectable evidence for the debug Manual Redirect Defense case
Inject one changed Go failure, verify bounded cleanup, and state live-network transfer limits for the debug Manual Redirect Defense case

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Choose automatic, manual, or rejected redirects from method replay, SSRF, credential, audit, and user-consent risk. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.

hints: For Go Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Manual Redirect Defense precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Go Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: Go incident collection agent sends POST G-3 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Choose automatic, manual, or rejected redirects from method replay, SSRF, credential, audit, and user-consent risk. Demonstrate the result through Go 1.26 net/http, net/url, context, io, crypto/tls, RoundTripper, and httptest evidence.

For Go Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Following redirects is transport plumbing with no application or security semantics. This remains false for Go 1.26 net/http.
